commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Human maintainer/security review is needed because the PR changes a compatibility-sensitive Gateway auth boundary, still has a concrete RPC blocker, and requires contributor-owned real behavior proof.; Needs attention: No supply-chain concern was found, but the auth/tool boundary change currently conflates direct HTTP with RPC and needs correction before merge.; Review finding: [P1] Keep the owner gate off RP... (sha=de55537cb118530a65506a63158add2e3c42d946)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds an owner-only deny list for `cron`, `gateway`, and `nodes` in Gateway tool resolution and adds regression tests for non-owner HTTP denial, shared-secret access, and inherited denies.

PR surface: Source +16, Tests +75. Total +91 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection: configure `gateway.tools.allow` and agent policy for `nodes`, then call Gateway RPC `tools.invoke`; the RPC adapter passes no `senderIsOwner` and the shared resolver still uses `surface: "http"`, so the new deny filters the tool.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human maintainer/security review is needed because the PR changes a compatibility-sensitive Gateway auth boundary, still has a concrete RPC blocker, and requires contributor-owned real behavior proof.

target.title: fix: gate owner-only HTTP tools
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Restores sender-owner gating for owner-only core tools exposed through Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke`.
- Keeps `gateway.tools.allow` as an owner/trusted-operator override for the default HTTP deny list, but prevents non-owner identity-bearing callers from receiving server-credential gateway, cron, or nodes wrappers.
- Leaves shared-secret bearer behavior unchanged: possession of the gateway shared secret remains full trusted-operator access on this direct HTTP tools surface.

Why does this matter now?

- The direct HTTP tool surface already threads sender-owner identity through the shared invocation path; this makes that signal effective before tool execution.

What is the intended outcome?

- A write-scoped non-owner HTTP caller sees owner-only core tools as unavailable even if the gateway HTTP tool allowlist includes them.
- Admin/owner and shared-secret trusted-operator callers can still use explicitly enabled owner-only tools.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No config shape, migration, provider, plugin, channel, storage, or dependency changes.
- No changes to the Gateway RPC `tools.invoke` adapter.

What does success look like?

- Non-owner HTTP callers get `404 not_found` for owner-only core tools.
- Owner/shared-secret callers keep the existing explicitly enabled behavior.

What should reviewers focus on?

- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` applies the owner-only deny after normal policy allow resolution so `gateway.tools.allow` cannot reopen these wrappers to non-owner HTTP callers.
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` keeps the HTTP default deny and owner-only lists centralized.
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` covers non-owner denial and shared-secret owner behavior.

AI-assisted: yes.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

No public issue is linked.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Maintainer-requested security hardening for the Gateway HTTP tools boundary.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Yes, requested through the maintainer tracking workflow.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Write-scoped non-owner HTTP callers can no longer expose owner-only core tools through `gateway.tools.allow` on `POST /tools/invoke`.
- Real environment tested: Local source checkout with the Gateway Vitest wrapper.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.cron-regression.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: 12 test files passed, 160 tests passed.
- Observed result after fix: The focused regression tests get `404 not_found` for `cron`, `gateway`, and `nodes` as a non-owner caller, preserve shared-secret bearer owner access, and verify spawned sessions inherit the owner-only denylist.
- What was not tested: A live external trusted-proxy deployment was not started; the HTTP handler, auth method mock, and shared invocation path were exercised in the local gateway test suite.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: No browser/UI, channel, plugin, provider, migration, or storage path is involved in this change.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The prior code removed the default HTTP deny when `gateway.tools.allow` included a tool and did not apply any later sender-owner deny for these core wrappers.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.cron-regression.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added coverage that non-owner HTTP callers cannot access `cron`, `gateway`, or `nodes` even when both agent policy and `gateway.tools.allow` include them.
- Added coverage that shared-secret bearer auth still receives owner semantics for explicitly allowed owner-only tools.
- Added coverage that spawned sessions inherit the owner-only denylist from non-owner HTTP tool resolution.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The new non-owner owner-only-tool denial assertion would have failed because `gateway.tools.allow` removed the default deny and no owner-only deny remained.

If no test was added, why not?

- Regression tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Non-owner identity-bearing HTTP callers now see owner-only core tools as unavailable on `POST /tools/invoke`.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. This restores an auth/tool-exposure boundary for owner-only core wrappers on the direct HTTP tools surface.

What is the highest-risk area?

The main risk is over-restricting legitimate direct HTTP automation that relied on write-scoped identity-bearing callers using `cron`, `gateway`, or `nodes`.

How is that risk mitigated?

The restriction is scoped to non-owner HTTP callers only. Admin/owner scope and shared-secret trusted-operator callers keep the existing explicitly enabled behavior, and the regression tests cover both sides.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the PR and run CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI, automated review gates, and any maintainer-requested real-environment proof beyond the focused local gateway suite.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

None yet.
target.author: pgondhi987

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
